model: Xenova/multilingual-e5-small (quantized ONNX, mean pooling, normalized, query:/passage: prefixes)

chunks.text: Portfolio of YC Wang — aspiring game developer with a background in psychology, neuroscience, and biomedical informatics.
YUANCHEN WANG GAME DEVELOPER · USC MSCS THEME 中 / EN EMAIL LINKEDIN GITHUB ↑↓ NAVIGATE ↵ CONFIRM
3D Rendering Project Role: Engineer Tool: Unity URP, HLSL, C#, GitHub Duration: Fall 2025 · 1 semester

Unity URP — View on GitHub Why I wanted to get hands-on with real-time rendering beyond "using shaders" — specifically: how procedural textures, custom shading, and world-space masks can be composed into a readable VFX sequence. This was my final project for CSCI 580: 3D Graphics and Rendering (USC, Fall 2025) , where we explored both offline and realtime rendering techniques (rasterization, shading, noise/procedural generation, and pipeline concepts). What A real-time asteroid impact sequence in Unity URP: a procedural asteroid falls onto a terrain, an "impact frame" post-process sells the moment of collision, and an expanding shockwave reveals molten cracks across the ground. Unity URP HLSL C# Procedural Noise World-space Masks How My contributions (Terrain Transformation) I owned the te

L C# Procedural Noise World-space Masks How My contributions (Terrain Transformation) I owned the terrain transformation system — the lava/crack look and the reveal/fade logic that makes the shockwave "paint" the ground as it expands. Procedural lava & cracks shader (HLSL): Built a terrain material that blends from normal ground to molten lava using 3D Worley noise for organic crack boundaries. Corner rounding for Worley junctions: Standard Worley borders can create sharp, unnatural corners at cell junctions. I used the third-nearest distance (F3) to compute a corner metric and widen cracks near junctions, producing rounded lava "veins." Surface micro-detail: Added controllable normal perturbation (Worley + Perlin-style noise) to avoid a flat/plastic feel and improve lighting response. Dem

bation (Worley + Perlin-style noise) to avoid a flat/plastic feel and improve lighting response. Demonstration of the rounding corner effect and normal shading on the lava shader. (left: not rounded; middle: rounded corners; right: added normal details) World-space spherical reveal mask: Implemented a smoothstep -based mask using distance-to-impact-center with radius + feather controls, then used that mask to lerp between the original terrain and lava shader outputs. C# runtime control: Drove shader parameters every frame (center, radius, feather) using MaterialPropertyBlock so multiple objects can share a material while still receiving per-instance reveal behavior. Fade-out variant: Implemented a complementary transparent fade shader using the same world-space mask logic (useful for "clea

nted a complementary transparent fade shader using the same world-space mask logic (useful for "clearing" geometry inside the shockwave region). Controlled revealation of lava and fade-out shaders. Outcomes Team system context This was a 4-person project. While I focused on terrain transformation, the full sequence also includes: Procedural asteroid generation (vertex displacement + heightfield-driven normals) Velocity-reactive asteroid lighting (emission + Fresnel rim "burn") Impact frame post-processing (edge detection + radial blur + polar noise "speed lines") Shockwave bubble shader (depth intersection + Fresnel + heat distortion) What worked well Readable, stylized “lava reveal”: The world-space mask + Worley cracks made the shockwave feel like it physically alters the terrain. More n

rld-space mask + Worley cracks made the shockwave feel like it physically alters the terrain. More natural crack topology: Corner rounding reduced sharp triangular junctions common in basic Worley borders. Reusable control pattern: MaterialPropertyBlock-based driving is engine-friendly and scales to multiple affected surfaces. Limitations & next steps The micro-normal detail was tricky to balance; in the final render we reduced the detail scale to avoid noisy lighting. Next, I’d tune a sharper normal pattern that still reads cleanly in motion. I’d also like to expose a small “art direction” panel (crack width, junction rounding strength, lava pulse speed) for faster iteration. ← Back to Projects

avoid a flat/plastic feel and improve lighting response. Demonstration of the rounding corner effect and normal shading on the lava shader. Aegis Sword — a detailed 3D replication of the weapon from Xenoblade Chronicles 2
Aegis Sword Role: CAD Modeler Tool: Onshape CAD Duration: Spring 2021 · 4 weeks

Onshape CAD — View on Onshape CAD Why I wanted to practice translating a stylized game prop into a clean, manufacturable CAD model —especially handling asymmetry, layered details, and tight proportion matching from reference. This was my final project for EAS 143: Introduction to Solid Modeling (University of Rochester, Spring 2021) , where the goal was to build accurate parts and assemblies using parametric workflows. What A detailed 3D CAD replication of the Aegis Sword from Xenoblade Chronicles 2 , modeled in Onshape with an emphasis on readable silhouette, precise detailing, and assembly-ready structure. Onshape CAD Parametric Modeling Assembly Surface Detailing Reference Matching How Workflow Reference breakdown: Collected screenshots and orthographic-ish angles, then identified key p

rkflow Reference breakdown: Collected screenshots and orthographic-ish angles, then identified key proportions (blade length, guard width, handle thickness) to lock the silhouette early. Modular parts: Split the sword into repeatable subcomponents (blade core, guard, handle, emblem/ornament), so edits wouldn’t cascade across the whole model. Parametric feature tree: Used sketches + extrudes/fillets/patterns with named dimensions to keep the model editable during iteration. Design & detailing Asymmetry handling: Established a consistent datum setup and only mirrored where the design truly repeats; the rest was modeled intentionally off-axis to match the original. Clean edges & readability: Tuned bevels/fillets to avoid “too sharp” transitions while keeping crisp highlights on the main forms

ned bevels/fillets to avoid “too sharp” transitions while keeping crisp highlights on the main forms. Assembly mindset: Modeled as an assembly with part separation in mind (useful for future export, printing, or game-ready retopology workflows). Result The final model reproduces the sword’s layered structure and distinctive asymmetry, and the modular setup made it straightforward to refine proportions and surface details late in the process. ← Back to Projects

Onshape CAD Parametric Modeling Assembly Surface Detailing Reference Matching

How Workflow Reference breakdown: Collected screenshots and orthographic-ish angles, then identified key proportions (blade length, guard width, handle thickness) to lock the silhouette early. Modular parts: Split the sword into repeatable subcomponents (blade core, guard, handle, emblem/ornament), so edits wouldn’t cascade across the whole model. Parametric feature tree: Used sketches + extrudes/fillets/patterns with named dimensions to keep the model editable during iteration. Design & detailing Asymmetry handling: Established a consistent datum setup and only mirrored where the design truly repeats; the rest was modeled intentionally off-axis to match the original. Clean edges & readability: Tuned bevels/fillets to avoid “too sharp” transitions while keeping crisp highlights on the main

ty: Tuned bevels/fillets to avoid “too sharp” transitions while keeping crisp highlights on the main forms. Assembly mindset: Modeled as an assembly with part separation in mind (useful for future export, printing, or game-ready retopology workflows). Result The final model reproduces the sword’s layered structure and distinctive asymmetry, and the modular setup made it straightforward to refine proportions and surface details late in the process.
Automatic Differentiation Toolbox — a Python package for forward-mode automatic differentiation
Automatic Differentiation (AD) Toolbox Role: Python Developer & QA Lead Tool: Python, PyPI, GitHub Duration: Fall 2022 · 1 semester

Python — View on test.pypi.org Why Derivatives show up everywhere in optimization, ML, and scientific computing, but the common alternatives are frustrating in practice: numerical differentiation can be unstable (step-size tradeoffs), and symbolic differentiation can blow up in expression size. We wanted a small, readable tool that computes exact derivatives at runtime, so users can differentiate “normal Python code” without manually applying the chain rule. What A forward-mode automatic differentiation (AD) Python package built by a 5-person team . It evaluates user-defined functions and returns derivatives by propagating dual numbers through operator overloads and elementary functions. The focus was a clean API , strong correctness guarantees, and maintainability through a test-first wor

focus was a clean API , strong correctness guarantees, and maintainability through a test-first workflow. Python Forward-mode AD Dual Numbers Operator Overloading Unit Testing PyPI How My contributions (Core AD + QA) I implemented key pieces of the forward-mode engine and led QA to keep the package correct as features expanded. Forward-mode engine: Implemented dual number arithmetic and operator overloading to propagate derivatives through composed expressions. Function composition: Ensured derivatives flow correctly through chained operations (add/mul/div/pow, etc.) by applying the chain rule consistently across overloaded operators. Refactoring for clarity: Reduced redundancy and improved readability so teammates could extend the system without breaking core invariants. QA leadership: D

readability so teammates could extend the system without breaking core invariants. QA leadership: Designed and executed comprehensive unit tests (core ops + edge cases) to verify numerical correctness and prevent regressions. Debugging & integration: Coordinated module integration, triaged bugs, and documented design decisions to streamline collaboration. Release readiness: Helped ensure stable packaging/behavior for publishing on TestPyPI. ← Back to Projects

Cemented Dreams — Gameplay systems, combat mechanics, and level design.
Cemented Dreams — Gameplay Systems Role: Combat Designer · Gameplay Engineer · Level Designer Tools: Unreal Engine 5, C++, Blueprint

What Cemented Dreams is a third-person action game set inside a parasite-infested building. Players navigate an infected structure where a mechanical Hive grows through the architecture, creating traversal paths and combat spaces. I worked primarily on combat design, gameplay programming, and level design , building core movement and combat mechanics while coordinating closely with artists and producers. Gameplay systems were implemented using a combination of Unreal Engine C++ gameplay code and Blueprint-driven iteration . Main Achievements Designed and implemented core combat movement mechanics including grapple traversal and sliding combat . Built gameplay behaviors using C++ systems exposed to Blueprint for rapid iteration. Designed a level centered around a growing parasite Hive simul

sed to Blueprint for rapid iteration. Designed a level centered around a growing parasite Hive simulation integrated into the building structure. Collaborated with artists and producers to align gameplay mechanics with visual direction and milestone goals. Highlights UE5 Gameplay Programming Combat Systems Grapple Mechanics Movement Design Level Design C++ Blueprint How Combat Design + Gameplay Engineering I designed and implemented the player's core movement and combat systems, focusing on mobility and responsiveness in tight indoor environments. Implemented gameplay behaviors such as grapple traversal, sliding movement, and aim-assist targeting . Integrated player input, animation states, and physics interactions through C++ gameplay components and Blueprint interfaces . Exposed key comb

and physics interactions through C++ gameplay components and Blueprint interfaces . Exposed key combat parameters to designers through Blueprint to enable rapid tuning of movement feel and combat pacing . Worked closely with artists and producers to ensure mechanics supported the intended combat rhythm and player flow. Grapple traversal Aim-assist targeting Level Design — Hive Simulation I designed a level centered around a parasitic Hive structure growing inside a building, creating traversal paths and combat arenas through environmental transformation. The central Hive structure grows into the building while cables from the structure attempt to strangle it. At cable contact points, the Hive generates hexagonal platforms that players can traverse. Entrances into the Hive are torn open by

e generates hexagonal platforms that players can traverse. Entrances into the Hive are torn open by structural cables , creating layered vertical gameplay spaces. A critical injection point forms around a cable that pierces the Hive wall, granting players the grapple ability . This level structure links narrative worldbuilding with traversal mechanics, turning the parasite's growth into both a visual motif and a gameplay system. Visit the official game site → ← Back to Projects

Highlights UE5 Gameplay Programming Combat Systems Grapple Mechanics Movement Design Level Design C++ Blueprint

How Combat Design + Gameplay Engineering I designed and implemented the player's core movement and combat systems, focusing on mobility and responsiveness in tight indoor environments. Implemented gameplay behaviors such as grapple traversal, sliding movement, and aim-assist targeting . Integrated player input, animation states, and physics interactions through C++ gameplay components and Blueprint interfaces . Exposed key combat parameters to designers through Blueprint to enable rapid tuning of movement feel and combat pacing . Worked closely with artists and producers to ensure mechanics supported the intended combat rhythm and player flow. Grapple traversal Aim-assist targeting Level Design — Hive Simulation I designed a level centered around a parasitic Hive structure growing inside a

ign — Hive Simulation I designed a level centered around a parasitic Hive structure growing inside a building, creating traversal paths and combat arenas through environmental transformation. The central Hive structure grows into the building while cables from the structure attempt to strangle it. At cable contact points, the Hive generates hexagonal platforms that players can traverse. Entrances into the Hive are torn open by structural cables , creating layered vertical gameplay spaces. A critical injection point forms around a cable that pierces the Hive wall, granting players the grapple ability . This level structure links narrative worldbuilding with traversal mechanics, turning the parasite's growth into both a visual motif and a gameplay system.
Codebreaker — gameplay system architecture and combat mechanics.
Codebreaker — Gameplay Systems Role: Gameplay Engineer · Systems Designer Tools: Unity 6, C#

What Codebreaker is a systems-driven action game developed in Unity, focused on combat interactions and player decision-making in structured environments. I served as the primary gameplay engineer for the project, implementing the core gameplay architecture and combat systems while working with production and audio teams to integrate game features into milestone builds. The goal of the project was to design gameplay systems that support clear player feedback, responsive controls, and consistent combat pacing. Main Achievements Implemented core gameplay systems including combat mechanics, player interactions, and environmental logic . Designed gameplay architecture supporting modular systems and clear event-driven interactions. Integrated animation, audio triggers, and player feedback into

and clear event-driven interactions. Integrated animation, audio triggers, and player feedback into cohesive gameplay loops. Coordinated with production and audio teams to deliver playable milestone builds. Highlights Unity Gameplay Programming Combat Systems Game Architecture Player Interaction C# How Gameplay Systems Architecture I designed the core gameplay systems that drive player interactions and combat behavior. These systems manage player actions, environmental responses, and gameplay feedback. Implemented gameplay logic for player combat actions and interaction mechanics . Designed modular gameplay scripts to support flexible feature development and iteration. Integrated player input, animations, and gameplay feedback to ensure responsive controls. Structured gameplay systems so m

t, animations, and gameplay feedback to ensure responsive controls. Structured gameplay systems so mechanics could be extended without breaking existing systems. Game Feel and Player Feedback A major focus of the project was ensuring that player actions produce clear and immediate feedback. Integrated animation events and audio triggers to reinforce gameplay actions. Implemented feedback systems that communicate combat outcomes and player interactions. Worked with audio and production teams to ensure gameplay events triggered correct audiovisual responses. Level Interaction Design Gameplay spaces were designed to support combat pacing and interaction opportunities. Designed level interactions that encourage player movement and tactical decisions. Implemented environmental interaction syste

s that encourage player movement and tactical decisions. Implemented environmental interaction systems connected to gameplay events. Ensured gameplay systems supported designers iterating on level flow and player challenges. ← Back to Projects

CTIN 488 — Game Design Workshop (Fall 2025) This page documents my work across three studio projects in CTIN 488, a playcentric design course focused on rapid prototyping, playtesting, and iteration toward a clear player experience goal. The semester included an Up the River game mod/variation, a focused Mechanics project, and a team Final Project . Use the tabs below to see why we made each project, what we decided to build, and how it evolved through design photo journals. Up the River Mechanics Final Project Up the River: Variation / Game Mod Why Build a variation on a shared base game (Up the River) by changing constraints and mechanics, then iterate through structured playtests while keeping a specific experience goal in focus. What Our variation reworked the original linear track int

eping a specific experience goal in focus. What Our variation reworked the original linear track into a circular race-to-treasure loop with environmental tiles (Wave/Reef), Pirate hazards, and a card system that lets players speed up their ship or hinder others. The intended experience was pressure + strategy as players chase points while avoiding danger. How (weekly iteration) Week 1 — Core loop + first pass balancing (9/21–9/23) Defined the core loop: travel between Port and Treasure Island on a circular track, using dice + event cards to create risk/reward decisions. Iterated quickly on pacing and probability: expanded the board, switched from D4 to D6, increased pirate pressure, and tightened card/rule wording so turns stayed snappy (we focused on pacing/balance tuning + clarity). Week

tened card/rule wording so turns stayed snappy (we focused on pacing/balance tuning + clarity). Week 2 — Playtest-driven clarity + V2 polish (9/24–9/30) Ran a lab playtest and rewrote setup/turn order text for clarity; tuned incentives (e.g., when card draws trigger) to reduce “dead” rolls. Conducted internal playtests to reduce downtime and edge-case confusion (reshuffle rules, clearer card effects, max hand size), then consolidated everything into a cleaner V2 with a faster, smoother flow (we led setup/turn-order + edge-case clarity fixes and helped consolidate the final V2 rules). This project was selected for in-class presentation. Mechanics Project: Focused Mechanic Exploration Why Practice mechanics-first design: choose a core interaction, prototype fast, and use formal feedback to r

tice mechanics-first design: choose a core interaction, prototype fast, and use formal feedback to refine clarity, pacing, and player interpretation. This project started from randomly drawn topics . Our team selected four key words — auction , versus , romance , and solution-finding — and our goal was to turn them into a playable game. We specifically wanted to explore how auctions can function as both a competitive system and a narrative device, letting emotional stakes emerge from mechanical choices (instead of scripted story beats). What Too Bird To Handle is a silent-coordination bidding game themed around birds courting. Players coordinate without speaking, trying to read intention and manage risk through a “give or take” style action loop. Built around an auction-style interaction,

manage risk through a “give or take” style action loop. Built around an auction-style interaction, the experience emphasizes reading intent , managing risk, and negotiating value under social pressure. How (weekly iteration) Week 1 — Prompt → playable prototype + first external tests (Week 9) Used a “yes-and” brainstorm to interpret the prompt, then committed to an auction-inspired , four-player cooperative direction about birds searching for mates. Built a minimum playable prototype: sketched resource and bird cards, established clear win/loss conditions, and iterated internally to smooth the difficulty curve (less swingy, more teamwork). Ran early external playtests and captured the biggest issues: players misread the game as competitive, terminology (“bid/bird”) was confusing, and setu

st issues: players misread the game as competitive, terminology (“bid/bird”) was confusing, and setup/visibility needed cleaner structure. Turned playtest feedback into concrete fixes (terminology, setup clarity, table layout), and tightened win/loss wording for faster onboarding. Week 2 — Clarity pass + visual polish + finalization (Week 10) Ran additional external playtests that confirmed stronger engagement and pacing, while still highlighting edge-case questions and moments where the rules needed explicit guidance. Validated changes through internal playtests, then did a final consistency/clarity pass across the rulebook, cards, and board to remove edge cases and improve readability (so the experience matched the intended “silent teamwork” tension). This project was selected for in-cla

the experience matched the intended “silent teamwork” tension). This project was selected for in-class presentation. Final Project: Original Team Game; Role: Producer Why The final project is a free project : define an original experience goal, build an end-to-end prototype, and demonstrate playcentric iteration across multiple weeks (rules, components, balance, and presentation). What Our team built a competitive survival game centered on social hierarchy . Players take on different roles (upper- to lower-class) and must meet role-specific needs inspired by Maslow’s hierarchy while dealing with threats, deception, and negotiation as resources flow from top to bottom each round. How (weekly iteration) Week 1 — Core loop + first playable: We defined the concept, scope, and primary mechanics

iteration) Week 1 — Core loop + first playable: We defined the concept, scope, and primary mechanics (roles + descending resources), aligned on the intended player experience, and built an early prototype for quick internal/external playtests. We helped lock the core loop and tightened the threat / gun rules based on early feedback. Week 2 — Clarity + agency pass: We expanded and stabilized systems through playtesting, then improved readability and decision-making: “pet” → love , shifted to a single-winner victory condition, increased lower-class agency (starting hand), and refined the rulebook for fewer edge-case questions. We drove rule/wording cleanup and helped implement the agency + win-condition revisions. Week 3 — Balance with simulation support: We ran a focused balance pass suppor

-condition revisions. Week 3 — Balance with simulation support: We ran a focused balance pass supported by 200k-trial simulations , adjusted deck composition, and updated win conditions to reduce “accidental” wins, then validated changes via final playtests. We ran the simulation-backed balance checks and translated results into concrete deck/win-condition updates. Week 4 — Presentation polish: Finalized the slide deck and recorded the final presentation video. We supported deck polish and final deliverable prep. ← Back to Projects

Mechanics Project: Focused Mechanic Exploration Why Practice mechanics-first design: choose a core interaction, prototype fast, and use formal feedback to refine clarity, pacing, and player interpretation. This project started from randomly drawn topics . Our team selected four key words — auction , versus , romance , and solution-finding — and our goal was to turn them into a playable game. We specifically wanted to explore how auctions can function as both a competitive system and a narrative device, letting emotional stakes emerge from mechanical choices (instead of scripted story beats). What Too Bird To Handle is a silent-coordination bidding game themed around birds courting. Players coordinate without speaking, trying to read intention and manage risk through a “give or take” style

oordinate without speaking, trying to read intention and manage risk through a “give or take” style action loop. Built around an auction-style interaction, the experience emphasizes reading intent , managing risk, and negotiating value under social pressure. How (weekly iteration) Week 1 — Prompt → playable prototype + first external tests (Week 9) Used a “yes-and” brainstorm to interpret the prompt, then committed to an auction-inspired , four-player cooperative direction about birds searching for mates. Built a minimum playable prototype: sketched resource and bird cards, established clear win/loss conditions, and iterated internally to smooth the difficulty curve (less swingy, more teamwork). Ran early external playtests and captured the biggest issues: players misread the game as compe

rk). Ran early external playtests and captured the biggest issues: players misread the game as competitive, terminology (“bid/bird”) was confusing, and setup/visibility needed cleaner structure. Turned playtest feedback into concrete fixes (terminology, setup clarity, table layout), and tightened win/loss wording for faster onboarding. Week 2 — Clarity pass + visual polish + finalization (Week 10) Ran additional external playtests that confirmed stronger engagement and pacing, while still highlighting edge-case questions and moments where the rules needed explicit guidance. Validated changes through internal playtests, then did a final consistency/clarity pass across the rulebook, cards, and board to remove edge cases and improve readability (so the experience matched the intended “silent
board to remove edge cases and improve readability (so the experience matched the intended “silent teamwork” tension). This project was selected for in-class presentation.

chunks.section_title: This project was selected for in-class presentation.
chunks.text: This project was selected for in-class presentation.

chunks.section_title: Final Project: Original Team Game; Role: Producer
chunks.text: Final Project: Original Team Game; Role: Producer Why The final project is a free project : define an original experience goal, build an end-to-end prototype, and demonstrate playcentric iteration across multiple weeks (rules, components, balance, and presentation). What Our team built a competitive survival game centered on social hierarchy . Players take on different roles (upper- to lower-class) and must meet role-specific needs inspired by Maslow’s hierarchy while dealing with threats, deception, and negotiation as resources flow from top to bottom each round. How (weekly iteration) Week 1 — Core loop + first playable: We defined the concept, scope, and primary mechanics (roles + descending resources), aligned on the intended player experience, and built an early prototype for quick in

chunks.section_title: Final Project: Original Team Game; Role: Producer
chunks.text: ing resources), aligned on the intended player experience, and built an early prototype for quick internal/external playtests. We helped lock the core loop and tightened the threat / gun rules based on early feedback. Week 2 — Clarity + agency pass: We expanded and stabilized systems through playtesting, then improved readability and decision-making: “pet” → love , shifted to a single-winner victory condition, increased lower-class agency (starting hand), and refined the rulebook for fewer edge-case questions. We drove rule/wording cleanup and helped implement the agency + win-condition revisions. Week 3 — Balance with simulation support: We ran a focused balance pass supported by 200k-trial simulations , adjusted deck composition, and updated win conditions to reduce “accidental” wins, th

chunks.section_title: Final Project: Original Team Game; Role: Producer
chunks.text: simulations , adjusted deck composition, and updated win conditions to reduce “accidental” wins, then validated changes via final playtests. We ran the simulation-backed balance checks and translated results into concrete deck/win-condition updates. Week 4 — Presentation polish: Finalized the slide deck and recorded the final presentation video. We supported deck polish and final deliverable prep.
Gyrotris — a sci-fi puzzle game reimagining Tetris with rotating boards, dynamic gravity, and environmental hazards.
Gyrotris Role: Solo Developer Tools: Godot 4.3, GDScript, Piskel

Play on itch.io What Gyrotris is a sci-fi puzzle game that reimagines classic Tetris through rotating boards, dynamic gravity, and environmental hazards . The game is set on a spaceship trapped by a black hole, where the player clears unstable cargo while adapting to shifting conditions. Developed entirely by me for Untitled Game Jam #100 , this was my first complete shipped game . I handled the design, programming, and pixel art. Main Achievements Designed a twist on Tetris centered around board rotation and changing gravity . Built the full game solo, including game design, programming, and art . Implemented hazards such as board holes, falling debris, and limited repair tools . Shipped a complete playable prototype within game jam constraints. Highlights Godot GDScript Solo Project Puzz

complete playable prototype within game jam constraints. Highlights Godot GDScript Solo Project Puzzle Design Game Jam Pixel Art How Puzzle Design The core design goal was to keep the readability of Tetris while adding new systems that force the player to rethink space, timing, and recovery. Built classic falling-block movement and rotation as the foundation. Added periodic board rotation , which changes how players read and plan the grid. Used dynamic hazards such as holes and debris to disrupt standard settling behavior. Included a time-stop ability and limited repair robots to add strategic recovery options. Solo Development Because this was a fully solo jam project, I focused on building a clear gameplay loop quickly and iterating on the mechanic that made the game distinctive. Impleme

a clear gameplay loop quickly and iterating on the mechanic that made the game distinctive. Implemented the full game in Godot 4.3 using GDScript . Created the game's pixel art assets in Piskel . Balanced scope to ship a complete prototype rather than overbuild presentation features. Used the project to explore how established game formulas can be transformed into new experiences. Iteration Gyrotris also gave me an early foundation in iterative design: identifying which mechanic was novel, what players found confusing, and what should be improved in future versions. Evaluated clarity around board rotation feedback and readability. Considered future improvements such as stronger rotation animation and clearer hole behavior. Used playtesting feedback to think about how much chaos helps the
mation and clearer hole behavior. Used playtesting feedback to think about how much chaos helps the game versus hurts readability. ← Back to Projects

Built classic falling-block movement and rotation as the foundation. Added periodic board rotation , which changes how players read and plan the grid. .3 using GDScript . Created the game's pixel art assets in Piskel . Balanced scope to ship a complete prototype rather than overbuild presentation features. Evaluated clarity around board rotation feedback and readability. Considered future improvements such as stronger rotation animation and clearer hole behavior. Used playtesting feedback to think about how much chaos helps the game versus hurts readability.
Nothing Can Go Wrong — fast-paced game jam project built in Godot.
Nothing Can Go Wrong Role: Primary Programmer Tools: Godot 4, GDScript

Play on itch.io What Nothing Can Go Wrong is a fast-paced game created during a game jam, where the goal was to design and ship a complete playable experience within a very short development cycle. I served as the primary programmer , responsible for implementing the core gameplay systems, player controls, and game logic while integrating assets created by teammates. The project focused on rapid iteration and experimentation while maintaining a stable, playable build throughout the jam. Main Achievements Implemented the core gameplay systems and player control logic. Integrated game mechanics, UI feedback, and input systems using Godot. Rapidly iterated on gameplay mechanics under strict time constraints. Delivered a polished playable build within the game jam deadline. Highlights Godot GD

e constraints. Delivered a polished playable build within the game jam deadline. Highlights Godot GDScript Gameplay Programming Rapid Prototyping Game Jam How Rapid Gameplay Implementation Because of the short jam timeline, the main engineering goal was to build gameplay systems that were simple, modular, and easy to iterate on. Implemented core gameplay mechanics and player control logic using Godot and GDScript . Built systems that connected input handling, game state, and player feedback. Structured scripts so new mechanics could be quickly added or adjusted during playtesting. Rapid Iteration and Debugging Game jams require constant iteration and quick problem solving. The development process involved frequent playtesting and adjustment to ensure the game remained fun and stable. Itera

ocess involved frequent playtesting and adjustment to ensure the game remained fun and stable. Iterated on gameplay mechanics based on quick playtest feedback. Debugged gameplay issues and optimized scripts to maintain smooth performance. Ensured the game remained stable while new features were added rapidly. ← Back to Projects

Prime Engine — Engine Systems Implementation (frustum culling, physics/collision, animation blending, debug visualization).
Prime Engine — Engine Systems Implementation Role: Engine Programmer Tool: C++, Prime Engine

What This project involved modifying and extending Prime Engine , a minimal C++ game engine used for gameplay and rendering experiments. I implemented several engine-level systems, including: View frustum culling for rendering optimization Hierarchical BVH culling (static median-split and dynamic Morton-code trees) Collision detection and physics movement (move-and-slide with gravity) Skeletal animation blending (full-body, partial-body, and additive layers) Debug visualization tools for bounding volumes and animation state Working inside the engine required understanding its component system , event system , and scene graph structure in order to integrate new features without breaking the existing architecture. Main Achievements Learned and navigated a large C++ codebase for a minimal cus

xisting architecture. Main Achievements Learned and navigated a large C++ codebase for a minimal custom game engine. Implemented view frustum culling for rendering optimization. Built hierarchical BVH culling with static median-split and dynamic Morton-code trees. Implemented a basic physics system with collision and sliding response. Built a skeletal animation blending system supporting full-body, partial-body, and additive blends. Highlights Frustum culling BVH culling Morton codes Physics engine Collision & sliding Animation blending Additive layers Debug visualization C++ How Frustum Culling To reduce unnecessary rendering work, I implemented view frustum culling using bounding boxes. Steps: Compute the world-space bounding box of each mesh instance. Transform the box corners into clip

eps: Compute the world-space bounding box of each mesh instance. Transform the box corners into clip space using the projection–view matrix. Test the box against the six planes of the camera frustum. If all corners lie outside any plane, the object is marked as culled and skipped during rendering. I also added debug rendering for bounding boxes to visually verify the correctness of the culling logic. This significantly reduced the number of objects sent to the renderer when many objects were outside the camera view. Frustum Culling Pipeline Mesh Instance world bounding box Transform corners to clip space Frustum Test 6 plane checks Cull Render outside any plane intersects / inside Bounding box corners are transformed and tested against the camera frustum to skip off-screen objects. Physics

ox corners are transformed and tested against the camera frustum to skip off-screen objects. Physics and Collision System I implemented a simple collider system supporting sphere and box colliders attached to game objects. Each frame: Colliders update their world-space position from the owning object's transform. When an object attempts to move, the engine checks for collisions at the candidate position. If collisions occur, the movement vector is projected onto the collision plane, removing the component that pushes into the surface. The remaining movement causes the object to slide naturally along obstacles. Gravity For objects affected by gravity: Vertical velocity is updated each frame using gravitational acceleration. The vertical component of movement is applied to the object. If gro

sing gravitational acceleration. The vertical component of movement is applied to the object. If ground support is detected, vertical velocity resets to prevent continuous downward acceleration. This produces natural falling behavior while preventing objects from penetrating the ground. Physics: Move-and-Slide with Gravity Input Move desired displacement Apply Gravity update vertical velocity Collision Query check candidate position No Hit → Move Hit Surface remove normal component Slide move along tangent gravity Candidate movement is tested for collisions; if blocked, the engine cancels motion into the surface and keeps the tangential part for sliding. Animation Blending I extended the soldier's animation state machine with several blending modes , chosen at spawn via an npcType value fr

dier's animation state machine with several blending modes , chosen at spawn via an npcType value from Lua. The skeleton is split into an upper body (joints 2–37) and a lower body (joints 38–47), so clips can drive different parts at once. Full-body blend — two clips mixed by a factor (e.g. idle + run, weighted 0.5 each). Partial blend — a clip overrides only a joint range via PARTIAL_BODY_ANIMATION (e.g. upper-body shoot over a full-body idle). Additive blend — a clip layered on top of the existing pose instead of replacing it. For additive motion I added an ADDITIVE slot flag. The additive pass takes the clip's delta from its reference pose (frame 0) as a rotation-only quaternion and pre-multiplies it onto the joint's local transform — so a base motion stays intact while extra movement i

ultiplies it onto the joint's local transform — so a base motion stays intact while extra movement is layered on (e.g. lower-body run + upper-body shoot, running and aiming at once). Animation Blending Modes Full-body blend STAND + RUN 0.5 / 0.5 across all joints Partial blend Shoot (2–37) STAND (lower) upper-body override replaces joints 2–37 Additive blend STAND + Shoot RUN (38–47) layered on top delta pose added to upper body Hierarchical Culling — Static & Dynamic BVH I extended frustum culling into a Bounding Volume Hierarchy managed by a central CullingManager , so large groups of objects can be rejected with a few AABB-vs-frustum tests instead of one test per mesh. Each instance is bucketed as static or dynamic . Static BVH — built once, recursively median-split along the widest cen

cketed as static or dynamic . Static BVH — built once, recursively median-split along the widest centroid axis, with leaves holding ≤ 8 instances. Traversal prunes whole subtrees whose bounds miss the frustum. Dynamic BVH — rebuilt every frame for moving objects (to stress-test it I spawned many small meshes given simple random movement). Centroids are normalized and encoded as 30-bit Morton codes (Z-order), sorted, then split at the midpoint — a linear, cache-friendly construction. The static tree gives better quality for buildings and terrain, while the Morton-code rebuild is cheap enough to run per frame for many moving units — Z-order keeps spatially close objects adjacent in memory, so the build and traversal access nodes nearly linearly, staying cache-friendly. Both are roughly O(n l

the build and traversal access nodes nearly linearly, staying cache-friendly. Both are roughly O(n log n) , dominated by the sort. Static vs. Dynamic BVH Construction Static BVH — median split root AABB split on widest axis · leaves ≤ 8 instances built once for static geometry Dynamic BVH — Morton (Z-order) Morton code → sort → midpoint split rebuilt every frame for moving objects Static BVH — rainbow boxes from root (red) to leaf (purple). Dynamic BVH — white boxes over randomly moving meshes, leaves shown as dots. ← Back to Projects

What This project involved modifying and extending Prime Engine , a minimal C++ game engine used for gameplay and rendering experiments. I implemented several engine-level systems, including: View frustum culling for rendering optimization Hierarchical BVH culling (static median-split and dynamic Morton-code trees) Collision detection and physics movement (move-and-slide with gravity) Skeletal animation blending (full-body, partial-body, and additive layers) Debug visualization tools for bounding volumes and animation state Working inside the engine required understanding its component system , event system , and scene graph structure in order to integrate new features without breaking the existing architecture. Main Achievements Learned and navigated a large C++ codebase for a minimal cus

xisting architecture. Main Achievements Learned and navigated a large C++ codebase for a minimal custom game engine. Implemented view frustum culling for rendering optimization. Built hierarchical BVH culling with static median-split and dynamic Morton-code trees. Implemented a basic physics system with collision and sliding response. Built a skeletal animation blending system supporting full-body, partial-body, and additive blends. Highlights Frustum culling BVH culling Morton codes Physics engine Collision & sliding Animation blending Additive layers Debug visualization C++

How Frustum Culling To reduce unnecessary rendering work, I implemented view frustum culling using bounding boxes. Steps: Compute the world-space bounding box of each mesh instance. Transform the box corners into clip space using the projection–view matrix. Test the box against the six planes of the camera frustum. If all corners lie outside any plane, the object is marked as culled and skipped during rendering. I also added debug rendering for bounding boxes to visually verify the correctness of the culling logic. This significantly reduced the number of objects sent to the renderer when many objects were outside the camera view. Frustum Culling Pipeline Mesh Instance world bounding box Transform corners to clip space Frustum Test 6 plane checks Cull Render outside any plane intersects /

nsform corners to clip space Frustum Test 6 plane checks Cull Render outside any plane intersects / inside Bounding box corners are transformed and tested against the camera frustum to skip off-screen objects. Physics and Collision System I implemented a simple collider system supporting sphere and box colliders attached to game objects. Each frame: Colliders update their world-space position from the owning object's transform. When an object attempts to move, the engine checks for collisions at the candidate position. If collisions occur, the movement vector is projected onto the collision plane, removing the component that pushes into the surface. The remaining movement causes the object to slide naturally along obstacles. Gravity For objects affected by gravity: Vertical velocity is upd

o slide naturally along obstacles. Gravity For objects affected by gravity: Vertical velocity is updated each frame using gravitational acceleration. The vertical component of movement is applied to the object. If ground support is detected, vertical velocity resets to prevent continuous downward acceleration. This produces natural falling behavior while preventing objects from penetrating the ground. Physics: Move-and-Slide with Gravity Input Move desired displacement Apply Gravity update vertical velocity Collision Query check candidate position No Hit → Move Hit Surface remove normal component Slide move along tangent gravity Candidate movement is tested for collisions; if blocked, the engine cancels motion into the surface and keeps the tangential part for sliding. Animation Blending I

gine cancels motion into the surface and keeps the tangential part for sliding. Animation Blending I extended the soldier's animation state machine with several blending modes , chosen at spawn via an npcType value from Lua. The skeleton is split into an upper body (joints 2–37) and a lower body (joints 38–47), so clips can drive different parts at once. Full-body blend — two clips mixed by a factor (e.g. idle + run, weighted 0.5 each). Partial blend — a clip overrides only a joint range via PARTIAL_BODY_ANIMATION (e.g. upper-body shoot over a full-body idle). Additive blend — a clip layered on top of the existing pose instead of replacing it. For additive motion I added an ADDITIVE slot flag. The additive pass takes the clip's delta from its reference pose (frame 0) as a rotation-only qua

g. The additive pass takes the clip's delta from its reference pose (frame 0) as a rotation-only quaternion and pre-multiplies it onto the joint's local transform — so a base motion stays intact while extra movement is layered on (e.g. lower-body run + upper-body shoot, running and aiming at once). Animation Blending Modes Full-body blend STAND + RUN 0.5 / 0.5 across all joints Partial blend Shoot (2–37) STAND (lower) upper-body override replaces joints 2–37 Additive blend STAND + Shoot RUN (38–47) layered on top delta pose added to upper body Hierarchical Culling — Static & Dynamic BVH I extended frustum culling into a Bounding Volume Hierarchy managed by a central CullingManager , so large groups of objects can be rejected with a few AABB-vs-frustum tests instead of one test per mesh. Ea

groups of objects can be rejected with a few AABB-vs-frustum tests instead of one test per mesh. Each instance is bucketed as static or dynamic . Static BVH — built once, recursively median-split along the widest centroid axis, with leaves holding ≤ 8 instances. Traversal prunes whole subtrees whose bounds miss the frustum. Dynamic BVH — rebuilt every frame for moving objects (to stress-test it I spawned many small meshes given simple random movement). Centroids are normalized and encoded as 30-bit Morton codes (Z-order), sorted, then split at the midpoint — a linear, cache-friendly construction. The static tree gives better quality for buildings and terrain, while the Morton-code rebuild is cheap enough to run per frame for many moving units — Z-order keeps spatially close objects adjace

s cheap enough to run per frame for many moving units — Z-order keeps spatially close objects adjacent in memory, so the build and traversal access nodes nearly linearly, staying cache-friendly. Both are roughly O(n log n) , dominated by the sort. Static vs. Dynamic BVH Construction Static BVH — median split root AABB split on widest axis · leaves ≤ 8 instances built once for static geometry Dynamic BVH — Morton (Z-order) Morton code → sort → midpoint split rebuilt every frame for moving objects Static BVH — rainbow boxes from root (red) to leaf (purple). Dynamic BVH — white boxes over randomly moving meshes, leaves shown as dots.

Technical and research skills of Yuanchen Wang.

Skills Game Development UE5 Unity Godot C# C++ Programming & Tools Python Linux shell R C MATLAB Java SQL CSS HTML PHP Onshape CAD Robotics (basic) Docker GitHub Project management Research & Interfaces DPI eye-tracking (Dual Purkinje Image, Fourward Technologies) and VICON motion capture; HCI, data visualization, applied ML; Iterative playtest, focus group, usability studies Languages Chinese (Native) English (Proficient) Japanese (Elementary) German (Elementary)

Word Cloud QR Code Generator Word Cloud Generator Paste text below and click Generate. Text input Nothing Can Go Wrong is a design exploration in indirect control and consequence-driven planning. Instead of piloting the protagonist moment-to-moment, players design a route and then watch their plan unfold, for better or worse, as environmental objects react in surprising ways. The appeal—and the difficulty—comes from predicting emergent interactions while still feeling empowered by a plan that was yours. My responsibilities covered concept ideation, systems design, engineering, and integration of artist-created assets in Godot. The core loop began with a lightweight pathing tool, but the heart of the experience emerged from environmental 'verbs': train tracks, whirlpools, spring pads, trapd

t of the experience emerged from environmental 'verbs': train tracks, whirlpools, spring pads, trapdoors, and chain reactions that could delight or derail. Balancing readability with surprise required strict rules and effective feedback animation that help the player learn the world's grammar. Difficulty tuning was driven by data rather than guesswork; I exposed parameters for moving speeds, impulse strength, and trigger sequencing, allowing tight loops of test–tweak–test within the jam's limited timeframe. The art pass focused on clarity and charm: silhouettes that read at a glance, restrained palettes, and motion that communicates function. We achieved a polished, playable demo on schedule and ranked in the top 20% overall. Post-jam, I'd extend the system with better text and visual expl

and ranked in the top 20% overall. Post-jam, I'd extend the system with better text and visual explanation so players can gradually learn the mechanics, add optional objectives for mastery, and implement robust replays to encourage sharing. Most importantly, the project validated a personal design interest: agency through foresight. When players laugh at a plan that almost worked—and instantly want to iterate—you've got a loop worth growing. Resume / CV highlights of Yuanchen (YC) Wang: game developer pursuing an M.S. in Computer Science (Game Development) at the University of Southern California (2025–2027). Hands-on experience across game design and engineering: combat design and level design on Cemented Dreams (third-person platformer — grapple traversal, combat systems, Hive level), engine programming in C++ on Prime Engine (view frustum culling, physics collision and sliding response, debug visualization), gameplay design and programming on Nothing Can Go Wrong, Code Breaker, and Gyrotris. Toolset: Unreal Engine 5, Unity, Godot, C++, C#, Python. Career background before games: research in neuroscience, psychology, and biomedical informatics with peer-reviewed publications. chunks.page_title: AI chat agent project (LLM, RAG, agents)
chunks.section_title: AI chat agent project (LLM, RAG, agents)
chunks.text: LLM and AI agent engineering: YC designed and built the AI chat agent on this website end-to-end. It is a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system: site content is chunked and embedded into a static vector index, sentence embeddings run in the visitor's browser (MiniLM via transformers.js / ONNX), retrieval is client-side vector search, and answer generation uses an LLM served through a Cloudflare Worker. The system includes role-conditioned prompting, prompt-injection and off-topic defenses, per-turn logging for evaluation, and a tested Python ingestion pipeline. It demonstrates practical experience with LLM applications, embeddings, semantic search, and agent-style system design.

Machine learning background: ML and applied Bayesian analysis coursework at Harvard Medical School (biomedical informatics master's), the Automatic Differentiation toolbox project (Python developer & QA lead), and peer-reviewed publications applying machine learning to medical imaging (ps-KDE, semantic segmentation of chest X-ray images, PLOS ONE; NeuroImage neurocognitive research).

Work experience and roles held on projects: Combat Designer & Level Designer (Cemented Dreams), Game Designer & Programmer (Nothing Can Go Wrong), Game Developer (Code Breaker), Solo Game Developer (Gyrotris), Engine Programmer (Prime Engine), Designer & Producer (physical game prototypes), Engineer (3D Rendering Project), CAD Modeler (Aegis Sword), Python Developer & QA Lead (Automatic Differentiation Toolbox). Research experience includes published work in NeuroImage, Physiology, and PLOS ONE (ps-KDE, semantic segmentation of chest X-ray images).
